description: When either path leads to the same city, the fork doesn't matter.

guides.text: The goal: $(A \lor B) \to (B \lor A)$. A [disjunction|disjunction] doesn't care about order — '$A$ or $B$' is the same as '$B$ or $A$'. But proving it requires a new tool.

The new rule is `or-elim`, also called **proof by cases**. If you have a disjunction $p \lor q$, and you can prove the same conclusion from either $p$ or $q$ alone, then the conclusion holds — no matter which way the fork went.
Start as usual for an [implication|implication]: assume $A \lor B$. This is the disjunction we'll eliminate by cases.
Now prove $B \lor A$ twice — once from assuming $A$, once from assuming $B$. First case: assume the left disjunct $A$.
From $A$, we need to build $B \lor A$. Since $A$ is our truth and we want it on the **right** of $\lor$, use `or-intro-r`. The left side can be anything — let it be $B$.
One case done. Now the second case: assume $B$.
From $B$, we need $B \lor A$ again. This time $B$ is on the left — use `or-intro-l` with $A$ on the right.

Now the payoff. $A \lor B$ at step 1. From $A$ we proved $B \lor A$ (step 3). From $B$ we also proved $B \lor A$ (step 5). Use `or-elim` with three arguments: the disjunction, the $A$-case proof, and the $B$-case proof.
